caveats: a: The frozen labor-share input l is missing because the government code has no comparable receipts denominator.
a: Public descriptions emphasize priority use cases and do not provide representative time allocation across the Armed Forces.
a: AI may increase decision quality, tempo, and mission capacity without reducing personnel hours proportionally.
rho: Public sources cannot reveal the full classified deployment portfolio or performance evidence.
rho: Pilot, strategy, and investment announcements do not establish scaled use or net labor savings.
rho: Adversarial environments impose security and assurance requirements beyond ordinary enterprise software.
e: The frozen n input is missing, consistent with the absence of SUSB private-firm coverage.
e: A large private defense industrial base exists, but its firms are classified under manufacturing, professional services, facilities support, technology, or other supplier codes.
e: Government-owned or contractor-operated facilities require entity-level review but do not create an ordinary private-firm population inside the stated industry boundary.
s5: A numeric zero would imply an observed probability for an eligible denominator that does not exist.
s5: Mergers among defense contractors occur outside this government industry and cannot be imported into s5.
q: Mission effectiveness, readiness, deterrence, and avoided loss are valuable but do not map to the commercial-retention primitive.
q: Defense contractors may retain innovation benefits under procurement contracts, but they belong to other NAICS industries.
d5: Budget cost is not a direct measure of constant-quality national-security service quantity.
d5: The cited FYDP predates later budgets, geopolitical changes, reconciliation funding, and supplemental appropriations.
d5: Classified missions and readiness quality are not observable in public spending aggregates.
o: This is a structural judgment rather than an observed operator-displacement rate.
o: Force-structure consolidation may reduce establishments without eliminating the accountable national-security operator.
o: Contracting and autonomy change task allocation but do not eliminate sovereign responsibility.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The in-code establishments execute sovereign national-security missions funded through congressional appropriations rather than recurring commercial fees paid by external customers. Contractors sell goods and services to defense agencies but remain classified in their private supplier industries, not NAICS 928110.
lens.excluded_activities: Private defense contractors, weapons and equipment manufacturers, private research and development firms, civilian security services, international affairs, civilian air-traffic control, military service academies, passive financing vehicles, captive private units, and shells.
lens.included_activities: U.S. government establishments of the Armed Forces and National Guard primarily engaged in national security, including military operations, military police, military courts, military air-traffic control, and military training schools other than service academies, assessed under the fixed external-service lower-middle-market lens.

a.bridge: Defense and GAO sources identify AI use in intelligence analysis, operational planning, background investigations, sustainment, business operations, policy, and autonomous systems, but do not measure the wage-weighted share of current not-yet-automated tasks across NAICS 928110. I map data fusion, document work, scheduling, logistics planning, maintenance prediction, surveillance review, training support, and routine investigations into exposed work while retaining command, combat, physical maintenance, field operations, human judgment, and accountable use of force; prominent AI use cases may overstate exposure across the full military workforce.
caveats: The frozen labor-share input l is missing because the government code has no comparable receipts denominator.
Public descriptions emphasize priority use cases and do not provide representative time allocation across the Armed Forces.
AI may increase decision quality, tempo, and mission capacity without reducing personnel hours proportionally.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted DOD task inventory by military occupational specialty and civilian series, with time spent on intelligence, planning, logistics, maintenance, administration, training, command, combat, and already automated systems.
a.rationale: The defense mission combines large information, planning, logistics, maintenance, training, and administrative workloads with physical and command-intensive work. DOD explicitly targets battlespace awareness, force planning, sustainment, business operations, intelligence, and routine investigative tasks for AI. The midpoint recognizes broad augmentation and selected substitution without treating personnel-intensive missions as automatable.

d5.bridge: CBO projects real DOD budget costs under the Future Years Defense Program, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the exact 928110 service basket. I use planned real funding as a quantity anchor and widen for force structure, compensation, weapons acquisition, geopolitical threats, supplemental appropriations, efficiency, and mission mix; cost growth can overstate service quantity when input prices or system complexity rise and understate it when productivity improves.
caveats: Budget cost is not a direct measure of constant-quality national-security service quantity.
The cited FYDP predates later budgets, geopolitical changes, reconciliation funding, and supplemental appropriations.
Classified missions and readiness quality are not observable in public spending aggregates.
d5.change_evidence: The latest Future Years Defense Program mapped to real force structure, readiness days, training throughput, missions, civilian and military staffing, operation and support outputs, and explicit efficiency assumptions through the target year.
d5.rationale: CBO reports that the 2025 plan would raise the real DOD budget by 1.9 percent through 2029 and projects longer-run cost growth, with most later growth in operation and support. National-security need is durable, but a five-year quantity estimate must allow force reductions, automation, budget choices, and rapidly changing threats. The base therefore tracks modest real growth rather than headline nominal spending.

caveats: The frozen n input is missing, consistent with the absence of SUSB private-firm coverage.
A large private defense industrial base exists, but its firms are classified under manufacturing, professional services, facilities support, technology, or other supplier codes.
Government-owned or contractor-operated facilities require entity-level review but do not create an ordinary private-firm population inside the stated industry boundary.
e.change_evidence: A formal NAICS revision admitting private defense operators to 928110, or verified evidence that the frozen population contains privately controlled in-code firms despite the government-only definition.
e.rationale: The official NAICS definition confines 928110 to government establishments of the Armed Forces and National Guard. The fixed lens requires privately controlled lower-middle-market firms selling recurring outsourced services to external customers, so no in-code establishment satisfies the firm and commercial-revenue conditions.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($57,589,436,492) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0000 (margins.json[92]: ESTIMATE: public administration — EBITDA is not meaningful for government units; margin 0 makes the n_band chain yield 0 by construction (recorded as a known gap)); revenue/employee $374,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at --); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: This is a structural judgment rather than an observed operator-displacement rate.
Force-structure consolidation may reduce establishments without eliminating the accountable national-security operator.
Contracting and autonomy change task allocation but do not eliminate sovereign responsibility.
o.change_evidence: Statutory and operational evidence that autonomous systems or private organizations can assume final sovereign command, use-of-force authority, and national-security accountability without an Armed Forces establishment.
o.rationale: Sovereign defense, command authority, lawful use of force, military justice, training standards, and national-security accountability require an Armed Forces operator. AI and autonomous systems can execute or support tasks but cannot replace the accountable governmental chain of command within five years. Contractors may perform support functions while public authority remains in-code.

caveats: Mission effectiveness, readiness, deterrence, and avoided loss are valuable but do not map to the commercial-retention primitive.
Defense contractors may retain innovation benefits under procurement contracts, but they belong to other NAICS industries.
q.change_evidence: A valid population of private, externally contracted 928110 firms with observable customer pricing and renewal terms, which would require resolving the conflict with the official government-only definition.
q.rationale: Commercial retention after contractual pass-through, renewal repricing, and competition is undefined for Armed Forces establishments funded by appropriations. AI may reduce public cost or increase military capability, but those benefits are not operator commercial margin under q.

rho.bridge: DOD strategy and operational examples show intent and selected deployments, while GAO reports investment and workforce-management gaps; none measures the five-year implemented share of the exposed opportunity across the industry. I infer faster implementation from dedicated strategy, leadership, investment, and mission pressure, then discount for classified and fragmented data, interoperability, testing, acquisition cycles, cyber threats, adversarial manipulation, reliability, responsible-use requirements, talent gaps, and human authorization; strategic urgency may overstate scaled production adoption.
caveats: Public sources cannot reveal the full classified deployment portfolio or performance evidence.
Pilot, strategy, and investment announcements do not establish scaled use or net labor savings.
Adversarial environments impose security and assurance requirements beyond ordinary enterprise software.
rho.change_evidence: A classified and unclassified portfolio audit reporting production deployments, user populations, mission hours covered, test and evaluation results, human-review burden, acquisition time, security incidents, model retirement, and realized labor avoidance by component.
rho.rationale: DOD has a formal adoption strategy, a central AI office, generative-AI task force, and active operational use. The strategy explicitly targets enterprise and warfighting impact, but GAO reports unresolved difficulty identifying and planning the AI workforce. Implementation should be meaningful in information and enterprise workflows while remaining slower for safety-critical autonomous and operational systems.

caveats: A numeric zero would imply an observed probability for an eligible denominator that does not exist.
Mergers among defense contractors occur outside this government industry and cannot be imported into s5.
s5.change_evidence: Evidence of privately controlled firms legitimately classified in 928110 and transaction records distinguishing company control sales from military reorganizations and procurement changes.
s5.rationale: There are no eligible firms under the frozen lens, so a probability of qualifying private control transfer has no defined population. Changes in command, military reorganization, base realignment, elections, appropriations, or contractor awards are not control transactions in an eligible in-code firm.

principal_driver: Defense has strong strategic pressure and dedicated infrastructure to deploy AI across information-intensive and enterprise workflows.
principal_weakness: Government Armed Forces establishments cannot supply the private control-transfer and commercial-retention opportunity required by the fixed lens.
